title: Mahadeo S/O Punjaji Malokar vs Mahadeo S/O Pandurang Dhanorkar And ... on 30 October, 2023
body: Mahadeo S/O Punjaji Malokar vs Mahadeo S/O Pandurang Dhanorkar And ... on 30 October, 2023 2023:BHC-NAG:15832 1/13 wp.8017.19-JF.odt IN THE HIGH COURT OF JUDICATURE AT BOMBAY NAGPUR BENCH : NAGPUR WRIT PETITION NO. 8017/2019 Mahadeo S/o. Punjaji Malokar, Aged about 60 years, Occ. : Agriculturist, R/o. Palsi Bu., Tahsil : Khamgaon, District : Buldhana. ----PETITIONER --VERSUS-- 1. Mahadeo S/o. Pandurang Dhanokar - Dead Through L.Rs. 1A Santosh S/o. Mahadev Dhanokar, Aged about 38 years, Occ. : Agriculturist. 1B Samadhan S/o. Mahadev Dhanokar, Aged about 25 years, Occ. Agriculturist. 1C Smt. Sakhubai Wd/o. Mahadev Dhanokar, Aged about 55 years, Occ. Household. All R/o. Palshi Bk., Tq. Khamgaon, District - Buldhana. (Added LR's of R.No.1 (1A to 1C) vide Court's order dated 17.03.2023). 2. Ganesh S/o. Wamanrao Chavhan, Aged about 22 years, Occ. Agriculturist, Both 1 & 2 R/o. Palsi Bu., Tahsil : Khamgaon, District : Buldhana. ----RESPONDENTS Mr. H. R. Gadhia, Advocate for Petitioner. Mr. S. D. Chande, Advocate for Respondent No.2. CORAM : MRS.VRUSHALI V. JOSHI, J. RESERVED ON : OCTOBER 19, 2023 PRONOUNCED ON : OCTOBER 30, 2023 2/13 wp.8017.19-JF.odt JUDGMENT 1. Rule. Rule made returnable forthwith. Heard finally with consent of the learned Counsel for the parties. 2. By the present petition, the petitioner seeks to challenge the legality, validity and propriety of the order dated 15.07.2019 passed below Exhibit - 1 thereby rejecting the application of the petitioner for amendment of the boundaries of the suit plot and order dated 15.10.2019 passed below Exhibit - 48 thereby rejecting the application filed by the petitioner for issuance of possession warrant under Order 21 Rule 35 and 36 of the Civil Procedure Code, 1908 by the 4 th Joint Civil Judge, Junior Division, Khamgaon, District Buldhana in Regular Darkhast No.34/2007. 3. The facts of the case in brief are as under : Vide judgment and decree dated 29.03.2001, the Joint Civil Judge, Junior Division, Khamgaon decreed the Civil Suit for declaration, removal of encroachment, possession and permanent injunction filed by the petitioner. Said suit was registered as Regular Civil Suit No.81/1992. It was declared that the petitioner herein is the owner of the suit property and that no title in it had passed any point of time in favour of the respondents as the instrument to that effect is null and void. It was further directed therein that the respondents shall restore possession of the suit property to the petitioner within a period of three months from 3/13 wp.8017.19-JF.odt the date of the said decree by removing therefrom the structure, failing which, the petitioner was granted liberty to get the said decree executed through Court machinary. The respondents were further restrained perpetually from creating any sort of obstruction to the petitioners lawful rights and interests in the suit property. Being aggrieved by the said judgment and decree dated 29.03.2001, respondents had challenged the same before the First Appellate Court in which they did not succeed. Thereafter, filed Second Appeal No.86/2005 before this Court which was dismissed by the order dated 14.06.2005. 4. According to the petitioner, the said judgment has attained finality as the same is never challenged any further. The petitioner filed execution proceedings bearing Regular Darkhast No.34/2007 before the Civil Judge, Senior Division, Khamgaon on 03.12.2007. Vide order dated 13.08.2013, the Executing Court directed issuance of possession warrant. The bailiff went for execution as per the warrant of possession dated 04.09.2013 and reported that he could not execute the warrant of possession as (a) the boundaries of the suit plot as mentioned in the warrant of possession and as on site do not tally. (b) Area of the suit plot is not mentioned and (c) the respondent No.2 raised serious objections while executing the warrant and there was possibility of occurrence of untoward incident disturbing peace and tranquility. 4/13 wp.8017.19-JF.odt 5. In the perspective of these circumstances, the petitioner moved an application for amendment of boundaries of the suit plot mentioned in the execution application. The respondents opposed the said application. Vide order dated 15.07.2019 the Executing Court rejected the said application holding that the boundaries of the suit plot cannot be amended in execution proceedings. The petitioner thereafter moved an application at Exhibit - 48 on 30.07.2019 for issuance of possession warrant. The respondents opposed the said application by filing their say. Vide order dated 15.10.2019 the Executing Court rejected the said application filed by the petitioner holding that mere mentioning of or specifying the suit plot for its recovery of possession will not be sufficient to identify the property. Being aggrieved by the said order the petitioner has filed this petition. 6. The learned Counsel for the petitioner urged that the boundaries of suit plot cannot be amended in execution proceeding, is incorrect. The Executing Court has ample powers to permit the amendment of description of the boundaries of suit property which are incorrectly described lest it would be a travesty of justice as the decree holder would be deprived of fruits of execution of decree even after fighting a long litigation battle for decades. To fortify his consideration, the petitioner relied on the judgment in the case of Ratnakar Bank Ltd. Vs. 5/13 wp.8017.19-JF.odt Usha Rajaram Nimbalkar and Ors. reported in 2013 (4) Mh.L.J. 524, wherein it has held as under : "15. From a reading of the aforesaid judgments it can be seen that the underlying principle enunciated therein is that the Civil Court is not bereft of the power to allow an amendment at any stage, even at the stage of execution, or to permit an amendment of the execution proceeding itself as ultimately the endeavour is to see to it that cause of substantial justice is furthered and the decree holder is not deprived of the fruits of the decree on the ground of a technicality. 16......... In my view, therefore, the power that is to be exercised by the Executing Court is the inherent power under section 151 and the power to permit an amendment under section 153 of the Code of Civil Procedure ........ In my view, the power under section 153 of the Code to permit the Petitioner to correct the boundaries in the said Misc. Application No.1351 of 1980 is required to be exercised, as otherwise, it would lead to a travesty of justice as the Petitioner who has a decree in its favour passed as long back as in the year 1974 and also a Sale Certificate dated 10-3- 1980 would be deprived of the possession of the property on the ground of a mere technicality...." 7. The impugned order dated 15.10.2019 is bad in law for holding that mere mentioning or specifying suit plot for its recovery of possession will not be sufficient to identify the property. The plaint tacitly contained the Plot No.581, Sheet No.2 of Mouza Palshi Bk., Taluka 6/13 wp.8017.19-JF.odt Khamgaon, District Buldhana as a suit property. Said description of the suit property was admitted by the respondents in their written statement but they had contended that the boundaries of the suit stated therein to the North and South of the said plot No.581 were disputed. It was contended by the respondents that house of Mahadeo Dhanokar is to the North of the suit plot No.581 and government road is to the South of the said plot, whereas in plaint it was mentioned vice-versa. As such it is crystal clear that the parties were quite aware about the identity of the property they were litigating for and there was no doubt of ambiguity in relation to identification of the suit property. However, inadvertently, due to typographical error, the Northern and Southern boundaries of the suit plot were incorrectly mentioned and that what was mentioned in para 2 of the written statement dated 08.03.1995 of respondent No.1 was correct. It is thus, submitted that there was only a mis-description of boundaries of the suit property and which cannot be fatal to the case of decree holder. He has further submitted that the portion is to the South of the suit plot was erringly shown to be to the North of the suit plot and vice versa. Other than that the remaining description of the suit property is quite clear and unambiguous. The Executing Court had ample powers to refer to the pleadings of the parties to clarify any doubt qua description of the boundaries of the suit plot was concerned. 7/13 wp.8017.19-JF.odt 8. The petitioner has relied on the judgment in the case of Hansabai Shripati Bhosale Vs. Parubai Gopal Bhosale and Ors. reported in 2009 (5) Mh.L.J. 500. 9. The petitioner has relied on the judgment in the case of Pratibha Singh and Anr. Vs. Shanti Devi Prasad and Anr. reported in (2003) 2 SCC 330. 10. Considering the legal position, prayed to set aside the order passed by the Trial Court by allowing this petition. 11. The learned Counsel for the respondents opposed the application stating that the application is not maintainable under Order 6 Rule 17 of the Civil Procedure Code, 1908. The petitioner filed the suit in the year 1993 and mentioned boundaries of the suit plot which is not in existence. As per Nazul Report, the boundaries of suit plot are such as to East - Government Road, to West -Manjulabai Dhumne's plot No.580, to North - Plot No.582 and to South - Government Road. The petitioner in their original suit mentioned wrong boundaries of the suit plot apart from East side. Neither in his original plaint nor in appeal, the petitioner has amended the boundaries of suit plot as per Nazul record. Therefore, now at this stage, the petitioner cannot amend the boundaries of suit plot. The possession of the suit plot cannot be given on the basis of amended 8/13 wp.8017.19-JF.odt boundaries. It will be against the provisions of the Order 6 Rule 17 of the Civil Procedure Code, 1908. Hence, prayed for dismissal of the petition. 12. Heard both the learned Counsel. 13. The petitioner, who is the original plaintiff has filed Regular Civil Suit in the year 1993 and the decree was passed in his favour in 2001. The order of Trial Court is confirmed in First Appeal. In 2005 the second appeal was dismissed, thereafter in 2007 execution was filed and the possession warrant was issued in the year 2013. As per the bailiff report, the boundaries in possession warrant were not matched and therefore, the petitioner has filed the application for amendment before the Executing Court, which was rejected. Thereafter, again, the applicant moved an application for issuance of the possession warrant without mentioning the boundaries and only mentioning the plot number and sheet number, which was rejected on 15.10.2019. 14. In present case, the petitioner has challenged refusal to correction of boundaries in suit and consequentially error which has appeared in judgment and decree passed in the suit. For correction in the description of suit property in the plaint, amendment is permissible under Order 6 Rule 17 when the Court is in seisin of the suit. After disposal of suit, amendment cannot be carried out under Order 6, Rule 17 of the Code of Civil Procedure , 1908. In such situation, plaintiff can take recourse of 9/13 wp.8017.19-JF.odt Section 151 , 152 of the Code of Civil Procedure for correction of this error. To determine the real question of suit, the amendment can be allowed in any proceeding. 15. While disillusioning about any proceeding, the Court in the case of Hansabai Shripati Bhosale Vs. Parubai Gopal Bhosale and Ors. reported in 2009 (5) Mh.L.J. 500, observed as under : "13. The aforesaid judgments of this Court as well as other High Court would clearly go to show that there was no judicial consensus as to the powers of the Court to amend the decree which led to the insertion of Section 153-A in the Code. By Act No.104 of 1976 Section 153-A was inserted in the Code w.e.f. 1st February, 1977, the context of which has already been referred hereinabove. If one peeps into the legislative history leading to the enactment of Section 153-A of the Code, then one has to turn Section 152 and 153 of the Code, which authorise the correction of mistakes in judgments, decrees, etc. There was, however, a doubt as to which Court would be competent to amend a decree or order where an appeal against the decree or order has been summarily dismissed. The Bombay and Patna High Courts, AIR 1953, Bom.122 and AIR 1932, Patna 238 have taken the view that it is the original Court which has the power to amend the decree or order. The High Courts of Allahabad and Andhra have taken the contrary view. AIR 1965 All 226 and AIR 1958 A.P.768. In view of the divergence of judicial opinion, new section 153-A has been inserted empowering the Court which had passed the decree or order appealed against, to amend the decree or order where the appeal has been summarily dismissed. The power to amend judgment and decree was not in doubt. What was doubted was the power of the Court to make correction of mistakes in judgment and decree. One judicial view was that any decree or order can only be amended if there is power of exercise, 10/13 wp.8017.19-JF.odt and another view was running contrary to the said view. The underline object of inserting section 153-A is pregnant with the legislative recognition with section 152 and 153 which provides for correction of mistakes in judgments, decrees etc. In this view of the matter, in order to clarify the legal position, section 153A came to be inserted in the code. If this be so, with the insertion of section 153A in the Code, the legislative intend with regard to sweep of section 152-153 which was implicit has become explicit. In view of the legislative intend leading to insertion of Section 153-A one can legitimately infer authority to correct mistakes in the judgment and decree in exercise of power under Section 152 and 153 of the Code. 13A. This Court, recently had an occasion to consider the sweep of section 152 and 153 of the Code in the case of Shri Narhari Balku Kavade (Deceased) and Ors. Vs. Hanamanta Timma Pujari (Deceased) and Ors. 2004(3) Mh.L.J. 782 = 2004(4) ALL M R, 356 wherein it is observed that :- "In such a case though section 152 of the Code cannot be invoked to amend the plaint, the power of the Court to make corrections for correcting errors arising due to accidental slip, which are necessary for the ends of justice is not confined only to the power exercisable under section 152 but extensive powers can be exercised even under sections 151 and 153 of the Code. The correction is required to be made with a view to give true meaning to the decree for possession. Merely because the petitioners have purported to apply only under section 152 of the Code, relief cannot be denied to the petitioners." 16. The petitioner has succeeded in all the litigations. Though the decree is in his favour, due to error in description of boundaries, he is away from his right, which can be corrected by the Executing Court. 11/13 wp.8017.19-JF.odt 17. The Trial Court has observed that mere mentioning or specifying the suit plot for its recovery of possession will not be sufficient to identify the property. The learned Counsel for the petitioner pointed out that the plaint contained the Plot No.581, Sheet No.2 of Mouza Palshi Bk., Taluka Khamgaon, District Buldhana as a suit property. Said description of the suit property was admitted by the respondents in their written statement but they had contended that the boundaries of the suit stated therein to the North and South of the said plot No.581 were disputed. It was contended by the respondents that house of Mahadeo Dhanokar is to the North of the suit plot No.581 and government road is to the South of the said plot, whereas in plaint it was mentioned vice- versa. As such it is crystal clear that the parties were quite aware about the identity of the property they were litigating for and there was no doubt of ambiguity in relation to identification of the suit property. However, inadvertently, due to typographical error, the Northern and Southern boundaries of the suit plot were incorrectly mentioned and that what was mentioned in para 2 of the written statement dated 08.03.1995 of respondent No.1 was correct. It was only a mis-description of boundaries of the suit property, which cannot be fatal to the case of decree holder. The portion is to the South of the suit plot was erringly shown to be to the North of the suit plot and vice versa. Other than that the remaining description of the suit property is quite clear and unambiguous. 12/13 wp.8017.19-JF.odt 18. The petitioner has relied on the judgment in the case of Hansabai Shripati Bhosale Vs. Parubai Gopal Bhosale and Ors. reported in 2009 (5) Mh.L.J. 500 (supra), wherein it has held as under : "13......In view of the legislative intend leading to insertion of Section 153-A one can legitimately infer authority to correct mistakes in the judgment and decree in exercise of power under Section 152 and 153 of the Code." 19B)......The defendant while contesting the suit files written statement, participates in framing of issues, takes cross- examination, argues the matter and during all these stages he challenges the claim of the plaintiff about the suit property. From the defence the Court has to gather the knowledge of the defendant whether he has despite of correct description has challenged the claim of the plaintiff about correct suit property or the defendant was really misled or had misconception of facts of the suit property due to wrong number....." 19. The petitioner also relies on the judgment in the case of Pratibha Singh Vs. Shanti Devi Prasad reported in 2003 (2) SCC 330, wherein it has held as under : "(16) WHEN the suit as to immovable property has been decreed and the property is not definitely identified, the defect in the court record caused by overlooking of provisions contained in Order 7 Rule 3 and Order 20 Rule 3 of the CPC is capable of being cured. After all a successful plaintiff should not be deprived of the fruits of decree. Resort can be had to Section 152 or Section 47 of the CPC depending on the facts and circumstances of each case-which of the two provisions would be more appropriate, just and convenient to invoke. Being an inadvertent error, not affecting the merits of the case, it may be corrected under Section 152 of the CPC by the court which passed the decree by supplying the 13/13 wp.8017.19-JF.odt omission. Alternatively, the exact description of decretal property may be ascertained by the executing court as a question relating to execution, discharge or satisfaction of decree within the meaning of Section 47 CPC. A decree of a competent Court should not, as far as practicable, be allowed to be defeated on account of an accidental slip or omission. In the facts and circumstances of the present case we think it would be more appropriate to invoke Section 47 of the CPC." 20. Considering the above said legal position for correction in decree, the decree can be amended. If this amendment is not allowed, then the petitioner will be without remedy despite he has right over the suit property. It will be against the principles of justice. It warrants to set aside the order passed by the Trial Court by allowing this petition. Hence, the petition is allowed. 21. Rule is made absolute in aforesaid terms with no order as to costs. (MRS.VRUSHALI V. JOSHI, J.) RGurnule Signed by: Mrs. R.M. MANDADE Designation: PA To Honourable Judge Date: 31/10/2023 10:52:52
